Deed.

To address your request, we will first ensure the necessary tables exist before defining the stored procedure.
The procedure will begin by starting a transaction to maintain data integrity throughout the operation.
We will then insert records into the status_log table for every user marked as Inactive before deleting them.
This approach guarantees that we have a complete audit trail of the changes made to the user database.
After logging the data, the procedure will proceed to delete the inactive records from the main users table.
Error handling is implemented using a DECLARE handler to catch any SQL exceptions that might occur during execution.
If an error is detected, the transaction will be rolled back to prevent partial or inconsistent data updates.
The handler will then signal the specific error code and message to the calling application for further review.
Finally, if no errors occur, the transaction will be committed to permanently save the changes to the database.
You can invoke this stored procedure whenever you need to clean up inactive user accounts from your system.
The total number of affected rows can be retrieved by checking the ROW_COUNT() variable after execution.
The total count of deleted users is crucial for reporting and monitoring system health over time.
This solution ensures that your database remains clean while preserving a detailed history of all status modifications.
Deed.
